coredata.dc:title: Preferential solvation and solvato(fluor)chromism of a green synthesized carbon nanodots in aqueous low molecular weight alcohols
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammad-Jafarieh, Parisa
Salamat-Ahangari, Rahman
coredata.dc:description: Solvent medium effects on the optical spectra of a carbon nanodot (C-dots) as a solvato(fluor)chromic indicator have been investigated. The molar fluorescence transition energies of C-dots in different aqueous low molecular weight alcohols (C1-C3) were interpreted in terms of the Kamlet, Abboud and Taft equation. Emission results demonstrate that acidity is the most influential feature for the case of lighter methanol and ethanol, whereas the polarizability of the mixed solvent was the dominant factor for the propanol. The preferential solvation of the C-dots under consideration in the mixed water/alcohol solvents has been studied and were analyzed using two different models. In words, solvent exchange process and an excess function model were employed to evaluate the extent of preferential solvation. Correspondingly, both models exploited, confirmed the tendency of C-dots to be preferably solvated by alcohols rather than water. Interestingly, with an increase in the length of the alcohol carbon chain the extent of preferential solvation illustrated a decreasing trend.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nano dots
Emission spectra
Solvato(fluor)chromism
Preferential solvation
Kamlet-Taft equation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Medium effect of water-(MeOH, EtOH and PriOH) binary solvent mixtures on the absorbance and emission spectra

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the research vice president of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant No. 601/97).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of C-dots in binary solvent mixtures of MeOH-water, at different mole fractions of water(magnified trend around the maximum points are illustrated in the inset).
Fluorescence spectra of C-dots in binary solvent mixtures of MeOH-water, at different mole fractions of water (magnified trend around the maximum points are illustrated in the inset).

vs. mole fractions of water (X0 1) for fluorescence in binary solvent mixtures of water-(MeOH, EtOH and PriOH), calculated; solid lines, experimental; points and ideal state; dotted lines.

Preferential solvation parameters of C-dots in water- (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH) binary solvent mixtures with different mole fractions.

head.ce:title: Preferential solvation and solvato(fluor)chromism of a green synthesized carbon nanodots in aqueous low molecular weight alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Maraghe Road, P. O. Box 53714161, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 35 km Tabriz-Maraghe Road, P. O. Box 53714161

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Maraghe Road, P. O. Box 53714161, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solvent medium effects on the optical spectra of a carbon nanodot (C-dots) as a solvato(fluor)chromic indicator have been investigated. The molar fluorescence transition energies of C-dots in different aqueous low molecular weight alcohols (C1-C3) were interpreted in terms of the Kamlet, Abboud and Taft equation. Emission results demonstrate that acidity is the most influential feature for the case of lighter methanol and ethanol, whereas the polarizability of the mixed solvent was the dominant factor for the propanol. The preferential solvation of the C-dots under consideration in the mixed water/alcohol solvents has been studied and were analyzed using two different models. In words, solvent exchange process and an excess function model were employed to evaluate the extent of preferential solvation. Correspondingly, both models exploited, confirmed the tendency of C-dots to be preferably solvated by alcohols rather than water. Interestingly, with an increase in the length of the alcohol carbon chain the extent of preferential solvation illustrated a decreasing trend.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Studies of solute-solvent and solvent-solvent interactions and how they affect the behavior of the solutes have attracted much attention because their interaction and their effects on all phenomena that occur in the liquid mixture phases have vast applications. In many cases, it was found that solutes properties depend on more than one solvent parameters [1]. The characterization of solutes in solvent mixtures is much more complex than that of the pure solvent. On the one hand, competition between the molecules of each solvent in the mixture with each other is a matter of debate in the solvent microsphere surrounding the species, and on the other hand, solvent-solvent interactions will strongly affect solvent-solute interactions [2,3]. Among the features of the water-alcohol mixtures, could be mentioned here are, a minimum in partial molar volume [4–6] and adiabatic compressibility [7,8], a maximum in excess heat capacity [4,5], ultrasonic speed [6,9] and a remarkable increase in light scattering [10] depend on the composition of water-rich or alcohol-rich regions. The aforementioned maximum or minimum thermodynamic and photo-physical behavior of alcohol-water mixtures tend to transfer towards lower alcohol or are influenced by the increase in the length of the alcohol carbon chain. Noticeably, a survey of literature indicate that at low alcohol concentrations, water molecules join to organize around the hydrophobic groups of the alcohol and behave differently from the pure solvent [11].

Due to the great importance and widespread applications of C-dots in the realm of chemical and biological, many studies have been conducted on the behavior of carbon dots in various environments. As a continuation of the investigation into the preparation and green synthesis of C-dots in this laboratory, the carbon dots used in this study was produced from persimmon peel, which is a green and safe bio-sourced, and to supplement more information about the solvatochromic properties of the luminescent C-dots in different solvents, we targeted the mixture of aqueous alcoholic solvents [12–16].

The solvatochromism properties of C-dots in binary solvents of (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water were studied by absorption and emission spectra, and parameters ET for emission as a function of mixed solvent composition properties and the effect of solvent polarity and special and non-special interactions were assessed. In this study, we focused on investigating the preferential solvation and solvatochromic behavior of green synthesized carbon nanodots in aqueous low molecular weight alcohols. The choice of these solvents was based on their relevance to real-world applications, solvating ability, and experimental feasibility. Aqueous low molecular weight alcohols, such as methanol, ethanol and iso-propanol are widely used as solvents in various fields, including pharmaceuticals, chemical synthesis, and material science. Their polarity and hydrogen bonding capabilities enable them to solvate a wide range of organic molecules and induce changes in the electronic properties of solutes. Moreover, these solvents are readily available, safe to handle, and compatible with the experimental setup and techniques employed in our study. Therefore, we selected these solvents to provide insights into the behavior of carbon nanodots in solvents commonly encountered in practical applications. To the best of the authors' knowledge, there was no published work on the preferential solvation (PS) of C-dots and from the previous study, it was found that the as-prepared C-dots provide an interesting system for investigating PS [16]. Hence, employing the intriguing emission behavior as powerful tools prompted us to model the preferential solvation of C-dots by solvent exchange model and excess function. In addition, the specificity of the two-component solvent was determined using the method introduced by Kamlet, Abboud and Taft (KAT) parametric equations.

The C-dots obtained in this research work have been synthesized using a simple and one-step hydrothermal method using persimmon peel as the main carbon source. Persimmon is prepared from a local market in Tabriz/Iran. The solvents used include methanol, ethanol and propanol with the highest purity available from Merck Company.

In this study, C-dots were fabricated from persimmon peel as a carbon source, their characterization and structures, were thoroughly described in our previous paper using the hydrothermal method [16]. Briefly, persimmon peel was dissolved in DI water (50 mL) and the solution for 15 min mixing with magnetic stirring, then the mixed solution was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 150 min. After the reaction, the reactors allowed were cooled down to room temperature in ambient temperature. The dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to separate the larger particles (supernatant containing the desired carbon nanodots remains in the upper layer). The resulting solution was adjusted to pH = 7 using NaOH (1 M) and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the C-dots solution was purified using dialysis against (1000MWCO) deionized water for 24 h. The synthesized C-dots powder was obtained using a lyophilization for 48 h, and for use in analyze a temperature of 4 °C should be kept in a dark container. C-dots characterization was performed using FE-SEM, TEM, FTIR and CHN analysis as described formerly [16].

Accordingly, for further spectroscopic analyses, C-dots solution was prepared in (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water binary solvent mixtures with a concentration of 0.1% (W/V) and a magnetic stirrer was used to obtain a homogenous solution for 4 h.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots in (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water binary solvent mixtures with different mole fractions were recorded by a CECIL CE7250 Spectrophotometer with a 1 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature over a wavelength range of 200–600 nm. Meanwhile, photoluminescence (PL) measurements of the C-dots solution were assumed using a Cytation 5/Biotek/USA fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation slit set at 1 nm pass and emission at 1 nm band pass in 96 cell plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Medium effect of water-(MeOH, EtOH and PriOH) binary solvent mixtures on the absorbance and emission spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The wavelengths of maximum absorption and emission of the C-dots in (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water binary solvent mixtures were studied. The spectra in Fig. 1 and in Supporting Information (Fig. S1 a and b) exhibit one broad absorption band in the wavelength range 250–320 nm. Due to the presence of water in all solvents, the result of red shift solvatochromism has been distorted. In addition, the resulting emission spectra display a splendid solvent-dependent shift as shown in Fig. 2 (and Fig. S2 a and b). In our previous paper, we attempted to allocate the medium effect in pure solvent on the absorption of C-dots to charge transfer amongst the C-dot and some evidence were presented [16]. Moreover, about the photoluminescence, the main reasoning was focused on the functional groups of the surface of C-dots as the emissive centers, in other words, treating the surface of C-dots as a discrete molecular fluorophores [16].

Upon comparison of the absorbance spectra of Fig. 1 (and Fig. S1) with emission spectra of Fig. 2 (and Fig. S2) the absorption bathochromic shift from the least polar (x1 = 0) to the most polar (x1 = 1) aqueous mixed methanol, ethanol and propanol are 6.5, 8 and 10.5 and the corresponding emission shifts are greater and are 13, 10.6 and 12, respectively. Hence the authors are legitimately, more concerned about the environmental effect of mixed solvents on the excited state electronic transition behaviors of as-prepared C-dots.

The maximum molar electronic transition energy values, ET, of each C-dots were calculated using Eqs. (1) in the studied binary water + alcohol solvents. In fact, specific and nonspecific interactions between the C-dots and solvent molecules are included in the ET values.
(1)
E T
kCal . m o l
- 1
=
hcN
A
λ
max
( n m )
=
28591
λ
max
( n m )
where h, c and NA are Planck’s constant, the velocity of light and Avogadro’s number, respectively. The λmax is the wavelength of the maximum of the emission spectra of C-dots in the binary solvent mixtures.

For a quantitative evaluation, the Linear Solvation Energy Relationships, LSER, for solvato(fluor)chromism of C-dots in the binary mixtures was exploited within the framework of Kamlet, Abboud and Taft equation.
(2)
XYZ =
( X Y Z )
0
+ a α + b β + s
π
∗
where XYZ in the present study stands for ET and α coefficient, indicate the solvent hydrogen-bond donor (HBD) acidity. The β coefficient indicate solvent hydrogen-bond acceptor (HBA) basicity and π* is the solvent dipolarity/polarizability of solvents. The regression coefficients a, b and s measure the relative susceptibilities of the solvent-dependence of the ET parameter. The maximum wavelengths of the emission spectra of C-dots were converted to ET by use of Eqs. (2) and the obtained ET were correlated with solvent properties employing Multiple Linear Regression (MLR) analysis.

The solvato(fluor)chromism parameters (α, π*, and β) of KAT values and the most significant resulting regression coefficients as the single, dual and multi-parameter correlation between ET for all the binary mixtures were obtained and are reported in Table S1.
The results and interpretations of the correlation of KAT equations for emission spectra of C-dots in binary solvent mixtures can be summed up as below:

The optimization results by considering all coefficients of α, β and π ∗ in the correlation analysis of the multi-parameter KAT equation with fluorescence data in binary mixtures were found that the best fit resulted were in the range of R2 = 0.99–0.98 coefficient in (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water binary solvent mixtures, which can be observed in Table S2.

According to the emission results obtained in Table 1 , representing the relative percentage distribution of polarity parameters, the displacement of carbon dot solvato(fluor)chromism in the methanol-water and ethanol-water solvents is affected by the parameters in the order of (Pα ˃ P π* >Pβ). In other words, this result implies that the acidity parameter plays a significant role and hydrogen bond donor (HBD) functional groups in C-dots lead to effective interactions with solvents capable of hydrogen bonding at the excited state. The influence of dipolarity/polarizabiliy and then basicity comes in the subsequent orders but for all cases, the sign of the adjusted parameters are negative. The negative sign of the coefficients means that an increase in the property of the solvents causes bathochromic shifts in emission spectra of C-dots.

According to the results obtained from the PriOH-water binary mixtures, it can be stated that solvent dipolarity/polarizability is the dominant parameter (P π*˃ Pβ ˃ Pα) so the nonspecific dielectric interactions of propanol on the stability of C-dot are substantial. The negative sign of the coefficient s again dictating the π∗ parameter property in emission gives rise to bathochromic shifts. In the previous paper considering the pure solvent, this stability of excited state confronting HBD solvent was in reverse order [16]. Furthermore, in all cases, by ignoring the acidity, basicity or polarizability parameter (α = 0, β = 0 or π*=0), the results could not be improved (not lesser standard error achieved), this case is in contrast with the results achieved in pure solvents [16].

One of the most successful ways to study preferential solvation is to use the method established by Bosch, Roses and co-workers [17,18]. The basis of their solvation modeling has been introduced following the Skwierczynski and Connors model based on two solvent exchange processes [19].
(3)
I ( S 1 )
2
+ 2 S 2 ⇌
I ( S 2 )
2
+ 2 s 1
(4)
I
S 1
2
+ S 2 ⇌
I
S 12
2
+ S 1
where S1 and S2 display the components of pure solvents in the binary water and alcohol system, respectively. In this research work, three types of homologues alcohol (methanol, ethanol and iso-propanol) with water have been used. S12 symbolizes the binary solvent mixture formed by the interaction between solvent 1 (water) and 2 (alcohol), I(S1)2, I(S2)2 and I(S12) display the solvated probe by solvents S1, S2 and S12, respectively [20,21].

The solvato(fluor)chromic property (herein, electronic translation energy) of the C-dot as a probe in a binary solvent mixtures depends on the composition of the solvation sphere treated as a local mole fraction weighted average of the ET values in pure solvents S1, S2 and S12 (ET(S1), ET(S2) and ET(S12), respectively).

The final equation in which the ET values of the probe could be correlated is as follow,
(5)
E T
=
E T
S 1
x
1
0
2
+
f
2 / 1
E
T
( S 2 )
(
x
2
0
)
2
+
f
12 / 1
E T
( S 12 )
x
1
0
x
2
0
(
x
1
0
)
2
+
f
2 / 1
(
x
2
0
)
2
+
f
12 / 1
x
1
0
x
2
0
where
x
1
0
and
x
2
0
are the bulk mole fractions of S1 and S2, respectively. The preferential solvation parameters, f 2/1 and f 12/1 measure the tendency of the probe to be solvated by solvents S2 and S12 in the preference of solvent S1, whereas the f 12/2 parameter measures the extent of preferential solvation of the probe by S12 relative to solvent S2.

The experimental ET values for emission spectra were correlated using Eq. (5) and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . For a better explanation, the dotted lines indicate the ideal state and it can be asserted that in the ideal state, there is no preferential solvation because of random mixing of components and the mixture of solvents surrounding the solutes (C-dots) are quite similar to the composition of a bulk solution.

A close look at the Fig. 3, shows a non-linear behavior of the molar transition energies and the evident deviation from the straight line for all binary alcohols reveals the preferential solvation of C-dots by one of the components in the solvents mixtures. The behaviors for optical emission charge transfer, in this case ET plots seem to have sort of sigmoid dependence on the mole fractions of water and is a role model of preferential solvation, in addition, no synergistic behaviors were observed for the emission results.

According to the results for the fluorescence data listed in Table 2 , the fitted parameters f 2/1 in all binary solvents are greater than unity (f 2/1 > 1) and their relative magnitudes are in the order of MeOH > EtOH > PrOH. The magnitude greater than unity demonstrates a preference for alcohol over the water in the cybotactic region of the dispersed C-dots. This magnitude decreases as the alcohol growing bulkier and one probable reason as discussed by Umadevi et al. [22] may be attributed to the greater dielectric enrichment in the peripheral of the probe by eliminating –CH2 group. Strictly speaking, for alcohol-rich regions for the mixed alcohol + water confronting the microenvironment of C-dots the hydrogen bonding among water molecules seemed to be predominant and hence it could have paved the way for alcohol to solvate the C-dots preferably (Fig. 4 ).

As it is obvious from the Fig. 3 and Fig. 4, in the water- rich region specially for propanol and methanol some disposition of preference to water over alcohols were seen and this can be attributed to intermolecular association of alcohol molecules. Correspondingly, for propanol the greatness of f 12/1 compare to the parameter f 2/1 is not negligible (f 12/1 > f 2/1) in binary solvent mixtures and in turn indicates the fact that the largest contribution of preferential solvation is from the complex alcohol + water (S12) system.

Having evaluated the credibility of the modeling described above, we made an attempt to quantify the extent of preferential solvation in terms of a different simple model, termed as, the local excess function as represented by Dawber et al. [23] Herein we follow the treatment of Chatterjee and Bagchi of Dawber model by employing the quantity [24];
(6)
δ 12
=
x
1
L
-
x
1
0
where,
δ 12
expresses the measure of the extend of preferential solvation and
x
1
L
stands for the local mole fraction in the microsphere of solvation of the C-dots and can be evaluated using the relation;
(7)
x
1
L
=
E
T ( S 12 )
-
E
T ( S 2 )
E
T ( S 1 )
-
E
T ( S 2 )

According to this treatment, the extent of preferential solvation is given by the extent of deviation from the ideal line. In this study, the results for C-dots dispersed in various alcohol + water binary solvents are plotted in Fig. 4 for fluorescence spectroscopy.

Taking into consideration the emission data of C-dots in the mixed solvent under study (Fig. 4 and Table S3), the results represent the negative values of
δ 12
for the entire range of the solvent compositions (Except for one or two points in the water-rich region of methanol and propanol). As conclusion, the excess function model is in good accordance with the former model and confirms hereby that the C-dots are preferentially solvated by the alcohols.

Lastly, within the framework of the solvent exchange model, taking a closer look at the high values of f 12/1 for mixed water - propanol, the C-dots probe was strongly solvated by the intermediate species S12 and this findings is quite similar to the behaviors of Betaine dye in the mixture of water and hydroxylic acids [25].

Moreover, for all cases for emission spectra, the parameter f 12/1 are considerably greater than f 12/2 and this could be indicative of the fact that the nature of S12 (water-organic complex) is closer to water with respect to the alcohols and is a reminiscence of self-association of water molecules. In addition, merely regarding the parameter f 12/1 and f 12/2 in exchange model, with increase in the length of the alcohol carbon chain the maximum values occurred in the case of propanol. Additionally, as quoted above, within the framework of Kamet-Taft equation different luminescent behavior in the aqueous propanol solvent was observed (greater value of dipolarity/polarizability parameter rather than acidity and basicity parameters) and is quite in consistent with the results extracted from preferential solvation modeling [17,25,26].

In this research work, the solvato(fluro)chromism properties of C-dots in binary solvent mixtures (MeOH, EtOH and PriOH)-water were studied. The results obtained from the absorption and fluorescence spectra showed a shift to longer wavelengths (bathochromic) upon increasing solvent polarity, i.e. C-dots could be treated as the positive solvatochromic indicator. In binary solvents, the parameters of π*, α and β, which were investigated using the linear solvation relationship of KAT, showed that the presence of all three parameters accounted for the best optimization, with the significant dependency on the acidity parameter for fluorescence spectra of aqueous methanol and ethanol and on π* for propanol. The preferential solvation evaluations were also studied by a solvent exchange model represented by Bosch and Roses et al. and were accomplished by one more approximate model termed as excess function. The superiority of the solvent exchange model has been reiterated and the results showed that the probe was preferentially solvated by alcohol rather than water and this fact was entirely consistent with the result of the excess function modeling. Additionally, with increase in the length of the alcohol carbon chain the decreasing trend of f 2/1 illustrates that the preference of alcohol over water was attenuated.

Parisa Mohammad-Jafarieh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Software, Visualization. Rahman Salamat-Ahangari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the research vice president of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant No. 601/97).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.122528.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
